coredata.dc:title: ZnO nanorods decorated with carbon nanodots and its metal doping as efficient photocatalyst for degradation of methyl blue solution
coredata.dc:creator: Roza, L.
Fauzia, V.
Rahman, M.Y.A.
Isnaeni, I.
Putro, P.A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report a facile and green chemistry technique to produce ZnO nanorods decorated with carbon nanodots (C-dots) to yield good photocatalytic for degradation of methyl blue. C-dots dispersed solution was obtained via simple carbonization of cassava peels at low temperature followed by ageing process for 6 days in absolute ethanol. The ZnO/C-dots heterostructure has been prepared by spin-coating C-dots dispersed solution onto ZnO nanorods surface. The impact of doping metal such as Co and Mn and the attachment of C-dots nanoparticles onto the surface of ZnO nanorods on their morphology, optical properties and photocatalytic activity were examined. The experimental results reveal that C-dots have successfully been attached onto the surface ZnO nanorods through strong physical interaction between C-dots and ZnO without modifying the morphology, surface area and crystallinity of ZnO nanorods. However, the energy bandgap of ZnO slightly decreases. This condition turns into increasing the charge separation efficiency of its electron-hole pairs which significantly enhances the visible light photocatalytic activity of the sample. The photodegradation rate of methyl blue was found to depend on the number of layers of C-dots. The Co doped ZnO nanorods coated with 4 layers C-dots particles was found to effectively degrade methyl blue solution compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. It is believed that high photocatalytic activity of metal doped ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite under UV light irradiation originates from their outstanding unique properties and the stimulated separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs based on C-dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Metal doping
Methyl blue
Photocatalytic
Ultrasonic spray pyrolysis
ZnO nanorods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Digital photographs captured (a) under visible, (b and c) UV light exposure of the cuvette containing C-dots sample prepared from cassava peels in daylight and dark condition.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (b) photoluminescence emission spectrum (λ of excitation-420 nm) (c) TEM image and (d) SAED patterns of colloidal C-dots.
UV–Vis optical absorption spectra of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure in the presence of various C-dots.
Band gap energy of heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample.
Photoluminescence spectra of ZnO/C-dots and metal doped ZnO/C-dots in the presence of 4 layers C-dots.
XRD spectra of ZnO nanorods and composite ZnO/C-dots with various C-dots layer.
FESEM micrograph of ZnO nanorods and heterostructure ZnO/C-dots with various C-dots layer; (a) Pure ZnO (b) 2 layers (c) 4 layers and (d) 6 layers.
Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy spectrum of ZnO nanorods and heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample with various C-dots layer.
(a) FESEM micrograph and (b) Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy spectra of heterostructure Mn doped ZnO/C-dots and Co doped ZnO/C-dots with 4 layers of C-dots.

Photodegradation rate of methyl blue under UV light in the presence of heterostructure ZnO/C-dots with various layers of C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photodegradation rate of Methyl blue under UV light in the presence of Co-doped ZnO/C-dots and Mn-doped ZnO/C-dots heterostructure with 4 layers of C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Texture Coefficient (TC) Value of ZnO nanorods composite ZnO/C-dots with various C-dots layer.

row.entry: Number of C-dots on ZnO nanorods surface (layers)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD data of ZnO/C-dots preferred (002) crystal plane.

row.entry: Number of C-dots on ZnO nanorods surface (layers)

head.ce:title: ZnO nanorods decorated with carbon nanodots and its metal doping as efficient photocatalyst for degradation of methyl blue solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pusat Penelitian Fisika, Lembaga Ilmu Pengetahuan Indonesia, Gedung 442, Puspitek Serpong, Banten, 15314, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pusat Penelitian Fisika, Lembaga Ilmu Pengetahuan Indonesia, Gedung 442, Puspitek Serpong, Banten, 15314, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report a facile and green chemistry technique to produce ZnO nanorods decorated with carbon nanodots (C-dots) to yield good photocatalytic for degradation of methyl blue. C-dots dispersed solution was obtained via simple carbonization of cassava peels at low temperature followed by ageing process for 6 days in absolute ethanol. The ZnO/C-dots heterostructure has been prepared by spin-coating C-dots dispersed solution onto ZnO nanorods surface. The impact of doping metal such as Co and Mn and the attachment of C-dots nanoparticles onto the surface of ZnO nanorods on their morphology, optical properties and photocatalytic activity were examined. The experimental results reveal that C-dots have successfully been attached onto the surface ZnO nanorods through strong physical interaction between C-dots and ZnO without modifying the morphology, surface area and crystallinity of ZnO nanorods. However, the energy bandgap of ZnO slightly decreases. This condition turns into increasing the charge separation efficiency of its electron-hole pairs which significantly enhances the visible light photocatalytic activity of the sample. The photodegradation rate of methyl blue was found to depend on the number of layers of C-dots. The Co doped ZnO nanorods coated with 4 layers C-dots particles was found to effectively degrade methyl blue solution compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. It is believed that high photocatalytic activity of metal doped ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite under UV light irradiation originates from their outstanding unique properties and the stimulated separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs based on C-dots.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalysis process has been known as a potential green and eco-friendly approach for treatment processes in removing toxic organic substance or hazardous materials persistent in our environment. The main component of toxic organic material which contaminated our environmental commonly comes from heavy metals, spilled oil, dyes, etc. [1–5]. Among the conventional methods for example adsorption [6,7] or membrane separation [4], the toxic substance degradation process through photocatalyst has emerged as an efficient approach to handle environmental contamination owing to their abundant, low cost and ease to use by simply adding the photocatalyst into pollutant solution [9,10]. This technique also gains more benefit because the catalyst can be reused by attaching the material serving as a catalyst onto substrate surface. Moreover, it can be employed under ambient conditions without secondary pollutant remains once the photocatalyst was added to solution [7]. Regarding to the above advantages, the glass slide as substrate which supports the photocatalyst not only offers easy and fast method for wastewater treatment but also meets for large‐scale synthesis for further development of sustainable applications.

Numerous metal oxide semiconductors such as TiO2 [8], ZnO [9,10], Bi2O3 [11], Fe2O3 [12] and SiO2 [13] have been utilized as photocatalysts. Meanwhile, zinc oxide nanostructures have been reported as an efficient and suitable photocatalyst due to their outstanding electronic and optical properties compared to TiO2 [14]. Besides, this material is also proven as non-toxic compound that is also physically and chemically stable with high electron mobility. It could be prepared via low-cost and facile synthesis techniques (for example hydrothermal or sol-gel method) along with their controllable morphologies. Contrary to these characteristics, ZnO based photocatalyst also has shortcoming properties due to its wide band gap bringing to low absorption in visible light region besides its photo generated charge carriers also quickly recombine, which restricts its extensive applications [15].

The photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanostructure with and without combination with other materials have been reported in many literatures [11–14]. Among numerous approaches, coupling with low band gap semiconductors, metal doping, incorporated on graphene oxide and coating or doping with carbon nanoparticles have been used as favorable approaches to improve photocatalytic activity of ZnO [16,17]. These approaches can extend absorption spectrum of the material mostly in visible-light region and facilitate high separation of electron-hole pairs [18]. Especially, the incorporation of carbon nanodots into ZnO based photocatalyst achieved the attention of researchers because of their unique properties such as excellent electron ability, strong photoluminescence, photo stability, biocompatibility, chemical inertness and high electrical conductivity [19].

Overall, the utilization of carbon nanoparticles (C-dots) to enhance the optical and electrical properties and catalytic activity of ZnO was found suitable as a green and environmental friendly approach [20]. Particularly due to ZnO and C-dots were known as non-toxic compounds, easy to synthesize with cheap preparation method and their sources of production are also unlimited. Moreover, C-dots have similar structure to graphite with high adsorption ability in removing pollutants from waste water. The formation of C-dots with the oxygen-containing functional groups in ZnO surface was found to be more efficient towards absorption of sunlight. The conjugated p structure of C-dots wellbeing to adsorb the organic pollutant since the functional group in carbon atom would facilitate easy to bind with other chemically reactive groups [21]. This induces the surface passivation and functionalization in catalyst to reduce various organic, inorganic, polymeric or biological materials in pollutant solution. In addition, the fast response of the sample to receive and transfer electrons, then stimulates the surface redox reaction in their surface become more effective if C-dots are applied as good co-catalyst to degrade organic pollutants. In other words, the C-dots act as electron reservoir to support the photogenerated charge carriers which can easily flow into ZnO and subsequently deteriorates the charge recombination in the sample [22].

In general, the charge transfer mechanism of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure based photocatalyst for decomposing organic pollutant can be simply explained. Once the ZnO/C-dots heterostructure is illuminated by UV light with energy equal to or higher than band gap energy, the absorbed photon creates electron-hole pairs. The electrons (e−) in the valence band of ZnO can be excited into the conduction band and the electrons move freely to the surface of C-dots layer through photogenerated charge transfer process. Meanwhile, the holes (h+) exist in the valence band once the electrons are excited to the conduction band. Subsequently, the h+ migrate to the surface of ZnO and reacts with water or hydroxyl ion (−OH) to form hydroxyl radical (•OH) in organic pollutant. These radicals are very active at attacking such organic molecules from methyl blue solution and then reduce them to more friendly substances such as CO2 and H2O.

In the present study, we have synthesized carbon dots via simple technique, namely, carbonization of cassava peels at low temperature followed by ageing process for 6 days in absolute ethanol. Carbon dots was then coated onto ZnO nanorods surface to form ZnO/C-dots heterostructure through spin-coating technique. The present method can also be used to produce metal doped ZnO/C-dots heterostructure. The novelty of this work is to synthesize carbon dots from fresh cassava peels and attach to ZnO surface to form ZnO nanorods decorated with carbon nanodots (C-dots). The first objective of this work is to investigate the influence of C-dots layers on the properties and catalytic activity of ZnO nanorods. The second objective is to study the effect of metal doping on the properties and catalytic activity of C-dots/ZnO heterostructure.

Analytical grade of zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O), zinc nitrate tetrahydrate (Zn(NO3).6H2O) and hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4) powder were purchased from Merck and used without further purification. Methyl blue was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water with 18.2 Ω was produced by a Millipore Direct-Q system. Meanwhile, fresh cassava peels were used as carbon source to synthesize carbon quantum dots (C-dots). Absolute ethanol was used as solvent to disperse C-dots and was purchased from Emsure. Glass substrate purchased from Gea Medical is cleaned using acetone and isopropyl alcohol in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min.

The carbon dots were synthesized through simple carbonization process of cassava peel in an electronic oven at low temperature followed by immersion in absolute ethanol for 6 days. Briefly, 100 g of cassava peels as the raw material was washed with distillated water to remove soluble impurities. These procedures were repeated until the last rinse of water becomes clear and colorless. A cleaned cassava peel was then dried in an oven and maintained at 220 °C for 12 h for carbonization process. Dried cassava peels were ground into a fine powder using a blender. Subsequently, the powder was filtered using 40 mesh sieves (hence, the grain size of dried cassava peels becomes ±425 μm). Ethanolic solution of C-dots with concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was prepared by adding 1 g cassava peel powder into 10 mL of absolute ethanol. The resulting solution was kept under stirring for 4 h at 1500 rpm. After being stirred, the dispersion was continuously aged at room temperature for 6 days until the dispersed solution was found visually to separate from other large agglomerated particles. In order to purify C-dots, the dispersed solutions of C-dots were carefully pipetted to eliminate the impurities and undispersed large agglomeration particles. Finally, the residual undispersed particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Hence, homogeneous clear and transparent yellowish-brown dispersed C-dots solutions were obtained.

An analytical grade zinc acetate dihydrate with 4.389 g was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water to obtain 0.2 M of seed solution. The seed solution was atomized to form a small aerosol droplet and sprayed directly onto heated glass substrate using ultrasonic nebulizer (1.7 MHz) for 15 min at 450 °C. The prepared sample was then annealed at 450 °C for 1 h and cooled to room temperature. The sample was continuously subject to hydrothermal treatment by dipping the glass substrate covered with ZnO nanoseed in equimolar of 0.05 M Zn (NO3).6H2O and HMT and kept at 95 °C for 6 h inside an oven. The prepared sample was cooled down to room temperature followed with rinsing with deionized water, dried with hairdryer and then annealed for 1 h at 450 °C. Meanwhile, the metal doped ZnO nanorods used in this work were prepared with similar procedure with addition of element Co and Mn as previously described in Ref. [23].

In a typical synthesis process, heterostructure ZnO/C-dots composite was prepared by two-steps spin-coating process for 6 s at 500 rpm and followed for 30 s at 1000 rpm by dropping 20 μl of C-dots dispersed solution onto 1 × 1.5 cm2 surface area of ZnO nanorods. The sample was then dried at room temperature for slowly evaporating the solvent, annealed at 50 °C on a hot-plate for 15 min and then cooled down to room temperature. These procedures were repeated with various numbers of layers, namely, 2, 4 and 6 layers to achieve the appropriate number of C-dots in ZnO nanorods surface. The samples were annealed at 70 °C for 1 h in air condition, using a hotplate and subsequently used as photocatalyst to degrade methyl blue solution.

The surface morphology and structural properties of ZnO/C-dots were determined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6510LA) and x-ray diffraction (XRD) Bruker D8 Advance equipment, respectively. An optical spectrophotometer UV–Vis Lambda 900 PerkinElmer, UV–Vis Diffuse Reflectance U–3900H Spectrophotometer and FLS920 photoluminescence spectrometer Edinburgh instruments were employed to study the optical properties of the ZnO/C-dots samples. Particle size determination was performed using particle size analyzer (PSA) Microtrac type Sync instrument. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of C-dots dispersed solution were recorded using a FEI Tecnai G2 20S-Twin, 200Kv electron microscope. All the characterizations were performed at room temperature.

To investigate the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods, ZnO nanorods/C-dots with variation of C-dots layer and metal doped ZnO/C-dots heterostructure, photodegradation experiment of methyl blue (MB) in aqueous solution were performed under ultraviolet light exposure in a photocatalytic chamber. Prior to the irradiation, all samples were immersed in 20 mL MB with the concentration 10 mM and kept under darkness for 30 min for adsorption-desorption equilibrium of the MB molecules on the sample surface. The photo degradation of MB with ZnO/C-dots was recorded using UV–Vis spectrophotometer after being irradiated with a UV lamp (20 W). After desired time interval, an aliquot of the solution was isolated and its absorbance was measured using UV–visible spectrophotometer to calculate the degradation. The degradation of MB under UV irradiation without ZnO photocatalyst was also observed as a baseline.

The physical appearance of C-dots dispersed solution prepared from cassava peels under visible light and irradiated with UV 365 nm laser can be seen in Fig. 1 . As can be observed from Fig. 1, the as prepared C-dots in absolute ethanol solution demonstrates a transparent yellowish-brownish color and has no fluorescence under visible light exposure. On the other hand, C-dots dispersed solutions clearly show fluorescence under UV light exposure by 365 nm laser, which indicates the formation of carbon dots. The C-dots sample emits intense blue-green fluorescence under UV light exposure due to their small grain size and different surface state of emission trap on the surface of carbon nanoparticles that could be called as quantum confinement size effect. This is in good agreement with other published data on ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite [24].

The absorption and photoluminescence emission spectrum of C-dots from cassava peels in absolute ethanol is shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), respectively. Meanwhile, the TEM image of C-dots solution is shown in Fig. 2(c). Commonly, C-dots dispersed solution exhibits the absorbance peaks at the wavelength from 260 to 360 nm in the range of UV light spectrum. It is found that absorbance peaks of C-dots dispersed solution appear in broad absorption peaks at the wavelength from 260 to 550 nm. The absorption peaks at 345 nm originates from electronic transition n-π* and corresponds to the C = O bond from the carbon core of C-dots sample and the transition from π-π* of the C = C conjugated bond from carbon surface of C-dots sample [25]. The present result indicates the successful formation of C-dots. The photoluminescence spectrum (PL) of the C-dots dispersed solution under excitation by 420 nm laser is given in Fig. 2(b). It has been observed that the emission peak intensity of C-dots dispersed solution centered at wavelength around 488 nm and indicates that the emission spectrum of C-dots dispersed solution shows strong blue-green emission. This result further confirms that C-dots has been successfully formed in as prepared sample which is in good agreement with physical appearance from C-dots dispersed solution under UV light exposure in dark as shown in Fig. 1(c). The blue emission from C-dots dispersed solution can be attributed from defect states from the structure of C-dots sample [26]. It could also be from impurity containing functional groups on the surface of C-dots sample [27]. The green emission is further recognized to sp
2 site from carbon networks which may be shifted due to size effects [28]. The TEM image (Fig. 2(c)) reveals that the distance of the lattice fringes of the C-dots sample is determined below 10 nm with good crystallinity.

The particle size distribution of C-dots dispersed solution is shown in Fig. 3 . According to Fig. 3, it is found that the C-dots particles demonstrate high dispersion, high homogeneity dimension with an approximate diameter of 4.8 nm. Furthermore, the poly-dispersity index (PdI) of the as prepared C-dots dispersed solution is about 0.346 nm. Low PdI indicates the particle size of C-dots is almost identical. This result proved the formation of C-dots since the material can be classified as carbon dots nanomaterial if their particle size in the range of 2–10 nm and demonstrates unique photoluminescence properties under UV light. The as prepared carbon dots exhibit normal distribution since the diameter of fine particles (smaller than 8 nm) and coarse particles (8–12 nm) do not have significant difference.

The optical absorption spectra of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure with various C-dots layer can be seen in Fig. 4 . The samples exhibit almost similar absorption characteristics with strong absorption in UV light region with sharp absorption edge at the wavelength 320–390 nm and weak absorption in the visible light region with wide absorption window area in the wavelength region from 400 to 600 nm. The absorption spectra at the wavelength about 320–390 nm is related to the characteristics of ZnO band gap energy where the sample absorb more photons in UV light region and then excites the electrons from the valence band to the conduction band of ZnO. The absorption spectra of all samples in the UV region are almost indistinguishable. Meanwhile, ZnO sample coated with 4 layers of C-dots exhibit the highest absorption intensity in UV light region. This phenomenon is ascribed to the fact that C-dots tend to agglomerate as the number of C-dots increase on ZnO nanorods surface. Furthermore, the absorption peaks of ZnO sample coated with 4 layers of C-dots also have continuous wide absorption in visible light region. The continuous wide absorption in visible light originally comes from the absorption of C-dots particles at their respective wavelength. The continuous absorption peaks in the visible light region due to attachment of C-dots particles on ZnO surface correspond to electronic transition n-π* and assigned to the C = O bond from the carbon core of C-dots and the transition from π-π * of the C = C conjugated bond from carbon surface of C-dots [28]. The prolonged of optical absorption and absorption intensity due to the presence of C-dot on the surface of ZnO nanorods sample allowed the sample to absorbs more photon. The presence of Co or Mn as dopant in ZnO extends the optical absorption in visible light region. This phenomena is associated to the formation of crystal defects in ZnO nanorods lattice as result of the substitution of Co+2 (0.72 Å) or Mn2+ (0.80 Å) with the ion Zn+2 (0.74 Å).

The Tauc plots for the optical bandgap determination of the samples are presented in Fig. 5 . It was observed that the optical band gap of ZnO nanorods samples was found to be 3.245 eV. Meanwhile, ZnO nanorods loaded with 2, 4 and 6 layer of C-dots dispersed solution exhibit band gap of 3.242 eV, 3.234 eV and 3.23 eV, respectively. The attachment of the C-dots on the surface of ZnO heterostructure slightly decreases the energy bandgap of ZnO. This condition turns into increasing the charge separation efficiency of its electron-hole pairs which significantly enhances the visible light photocatalytic activity of sample. The reduction of the optical band gap of ZnO/C-dots sample indicates the existence of C-dots particle in ZnO nanorods surface. Therefore, it can be recommended to utilize prepared sample as an effective photocatalyst in the visible region.

Fig. 6 shows the room temperature photoluminescence spectra of heterostructure ZnO/C-dots with various layers of C-dots, heterostructure Mn doped ZnO/C-dots and Co doped ZnO/C-dots. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample exhibits strong emission peaks in UV light region at 396 nm, multiple peaks at 435 and 473 nm and broad emission peaks at 650 nm in visible light spectrum. The emission peaks at 396 nm demonstrates that sample have high degree crystalline phase due to the free recombination of exciton in band gap of ZnO. The peaks intensity in visible light spectrum is generally due to native defect of ZnO. The peak at 435 nm may be originating from a different deep-level emission located in the band gap energy such as oxygen vacancies and/or zinc interstitials [24]. The blue-green light emission at 473 nm is attributed to surface defects in the ZnO lattice. Meanwhile, the emission peak at 628 nm is well known as orange fluorescence which is often associated to oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitials in ZnO lattice [16].

The existence of 4 layers of C-dots particles on heterostructure ZnO/C-dots surface increase the emission intensity in UV and visible light spectrum. It is revealed that all heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample present broad orange fluorescence centered at 628 nm. The heterostructure Co-doped ZnO/C-dots exhibits the highest emission intensity. These results indicate that the recombination of photogenerated charge on ZnO surface is low, in other words, that sample exhibits effective charge transfer electron-hole.

As can be observed from Fig. 7 , it is clearly observed that all the diffraction peaks corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110) and (103) planes can be assigned to the hexagonal structure of ZnO (JCPDS file no. 98-016-1836) [29]. Apart from ZnO/C-dots loaded with 2 layer and 6 layers of C-dots dispersed solution, the most intense diffraction peaks for all samples is located at the Bragg angle, (2θ) 34.42°, which corresponds to (002) wurtzite phase. From the figure, it is also obvious that the XRD pattern of sample gradually changed with the appearance of new diffraction peaks centered at 29.98° once C-dots dispersed solution loaded into ZnO nanorods. This peak corresponds to (002) plane of graphite and demonstrates that small quantity of C-dots particles were successfully attached on ZnO nanorods surface [30]. Moreover, it would confirm that carbon and ZnO nanorods having strong physical interaction and has insignificant effect on the crystal structure of ZnO nanorods.

Further analysis to identify the preferred growth orientation of the ZnO/C-dots with various C-dots layer quantitatively was done by computing the texture coefficient (TC) parameter. TC parameter of the sample taken from hkl for six diffraction peaks of the samples using the equation described in Ref. [18]. The values of TC are presented in Table 1 below:

According to Table 1, it is clearly indicated that the (002) crystalline plane for all samples have TC value greater than 1. The TC value for preferred orientation of (002) crystal plane was then used to further calculate the lattice parameters such as d spacing, crystallite size and FWHM. The parameters are presented in Table 2 .

From Table 2, it can be observed that the measured lattice parameters for all ZnO samples are similar, namely, a = 3.25 Å and c = 5.20 Å. These results are in good agreement with the standard data for wurtzite ZnO (a = 3.249 Å, c = 5.205 Å). The heterostructure with 6 layers of C-dots possesses the highest average crystallite size. This analysis further confirmed that the presence of C-dots on ZnO nanorods surface has insignificant effect on the crystal structure of ZnO.

The FESEM micrographs showing the morphology of ZnO nanrods and ZnO/C-dots heterostructure with various C-dots layer are shown in Fig. 8
. The average grain size of all ZnO nanorods sample was in the range of 109–184 nm, while the density of the sample is 137 rods/mm2. The morphology of all samples was found to be uniform throughout the substrate. As can be observed from Fig. 8, there are considerable differences between surface morphology and density of ZnO nanorods with and without addition of C-dots. ZnO without addition of C-dot dispersed solution exhibits high density of hexagonal nanorods and grown almost perpendicular to the substrate. Similar kind of morphology was also clearly observed on the ZnO/C-dots composite that was prepared with addition of C-dots dispersed solution. However, the growth orientation of sample is random or not exactly perpendicular to the substrate. The Brownian motion effect from C-dots dispersed solution as result of slow drying process when deposited C-dots into ZnO nanorods surface. Moreover, thin layer of C-dots dispersed solution was also found to cover the entire ZnO nanorods. Fig. 9 was used to show the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of ZnO nanorods and heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample with various C-dots layers.

The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of ZnO nanorods and heterostructure ZnO/C-dots sample with various C-dots layers are shown in Fig. 9. The EDX pattern of ZnO that was prepared without addition of C-dots dispersed solution shows the existence of the element of Zn and O. Meanwhile, the EDX pattern of ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite detects the existence of Zn, O and C element on the whole surface, confirming the presence of these elements in the sample. No other impurities are detected in ZnO/C-dots sample. It also shows that sample contains mixed carbon materials and ZnO nanorods.

The FESEM images and EDS spectra in Fig. 10 show the morphology and elemental composition of heterostructure Co-doped ZnO/C-dots and Mn-doped ZnO/C-dots, respectively. The samples have morphology of hexagonal nanorods. The average grain size of Co-doped ZnO nanorods sample is in the range of 47.7–235 nm and the density of the particle is 187 rods/mm2. Meanwhile, the particle distribution of Mn-doped ZnO/C-dots is found to be more uniform compared to Co-doped ZnO nanorods with the particle average size and distribution in the range of 87 nm–145 nm and density the sample is 167 rods/mm2. The EDX pattern of heterostructure Co-doped ZnO/C-dots nanocomposite detects the existence of Zn, O, C and Co element on the whole surface, confirming the presence of these elements in the sample.

The photocatalytic performance of all samples is shown in Figs. 11 and 12
. The methyl blue solution in the absence and presence of ZnO sample was kept in dark condition for 30 min to allow ZnO sample absorb the methyl blue molecule. The result clearly shows that the photocatalytic degradation of all the ZnO/C-dots heterostructures is considerably higher compared to pristine ZnO nanorods. Particularly, the ZnO/C-dots heterostructures sample with 4 layers of C-dots exhibits the highest activity due to 74.98% of methyl blue solution was found to be degraded after illumination with UV light for 70 min. The photocatalytic activity of sample significantly reduced as the number of C-dots increased on ZnO nanorods surface since the formation of agglomeration of C-dots would act as the recombination center of photogenerated electron hole pairs.

The photocatalytic activities of ZnO nanorods and heterostructure ZnO/C-dots samples with various number C-dots layers provides the information of the best content of C-dots to prepare the ZnO/C-dots heterostructure sample with good photocatalytic property to degrade organic pollutant. The degradation rates of the ZnO/C-dots, Mn-doped ZnO/C-dots and Co-doped ZnO/C-dots with 4 layers of C-dots are 75.02, 80.84 and 81.13%, respectively. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity of Co-doped ZnO/C-dots is attributed to the incorporation of 4 layers C-dots created favorable physical bonding with Co-doped ZnO nanorods, which subsequently encourages the efficient transfer of photogenerated charges at the surface of ZnO sample. As reported in the previous study, the conjugated p structure of C-dots was reported to adsorb the organic pollutant since this functional group in carbon atom would facilitate easy way to bind with other chemically reactive groups, inducing the surface passivation and functionalization in catalyst to reduce various organic, inorganic, polymeric or biological materials in pollutant solution [33]. In addition, C-dots act as reservoir of electron to give fast response of the sample to receive and transfer electrons, then stimulates the surface redox reactions at their surface [18,34].

The electronic interaction by chelation between ZnO and C-dots in ZnO/C-dots heterostructure was found to be essential to enhance the photodegradation rate of methyl blue solution. It is believed that the good photocatalytic activity of metal doped ZnO/C-dots especially Co-doped ZnO/C-dots sample under UV light irradiation originates from their outstanding unique physical property which stimulates the separation process of photogenerated electron-hole pairs based upon C-dots. This result is good agreement with the data analysis from optical properties (UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra) of the sample. The absorption spectra reveal that attachment of the C-dots on the surface of ZnO/C-dots heterostructure slightly decreases the energy bandgap of ZnO. Meanwhile, PL spectra indicates that doping ZnO with metal and the incorporation of C-dots particles increase the crystal defects in ZnO nanorods. This crystal defect will act as electron reservoir, which could prevent the recombination process of photogenerated charges and extend the electron lifetime which will play as an important role in the degradation process of methyl blue. In other words, increasing the charge separation efficiency of its electron-hole pairs significantly enhances the visible light photocatalytic activity of the sample. C-dots are believed as a light source for the excitation of electrons in ZnO, which then act as reservoir of electrons after excitation.

We have successfully synthesized carbon nanodots (C-dots) via a facile and green chemistry technique through simple carbonization of cassava peels at low temperature. The C-dots dispersed solution were coated onto ZnO nanorods surface to form ZnO/C-dots heterostructure and then used as good photocatalyts to degrade methyl blue under UV light illumination. The experimental results reveal that the incorporation of C-dots particles and doping with element Co or Mn into ZnO lattice modifies their morphology, surface area and optical properties. The XRD and PL analysis confirm that carbon and ZnO nanorods having strong physical interaction and has insignificant effect on the crystal structure of ZnO nanorods. The photodegradation rate of sample was found to depend on the layers of C-dots. The photocatalytic activity of Co-doped ZnO/C-dots heterostructure coated with 4 layers of C-dots particles has the highest efficiency in degradation of methyl blue solution.
L. Roza: Conceptualization, Methodology. V. Fauzia: Formal analysis, Data curation. M.Y.A. Rahman: Writing - review & editing. I. Isnaeni: Validation. P.A. Putro: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Hibah Penelitian Unggulan Perguruan Tinggi 2019 (No. 21/AKM/PNT/2019) from the Ministry of Research, Technology and Higher Education Indonesia.
